title: What the UK government should do on AI and tech policy
gist: Chatham House argues the incoming Burnham UK government should pivot from reflexive dependence on US technology toward 'tech sovereignty' — building domestic AI-deployment capacity, leading in digital identity and AI governance, and pooling resources with other middle powers rather than pursuing costly autarky.

themes.theme: US dependency and the sovereignty pivot

insights.claim: The UK's £150 billion Tech Prosperity Deal, signed during Trump's state visit, is reportedly now on ice and looks to be a high-water mark for US investment into Britain.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A stalled £150bn commitment signals that US-UK tech investment flows are politically fragile; investors positioned for a UK inbound-capital boom from that deal should reassess.
insights.quote: the Tech Prosperity Deal signed during President Trump’s state visit, which trumpeted £150 billion of future investment into the UK, looks now to be a high-water mark
insights.locator: US technology companies have invested heavily

insights.claim: The US has imposed temporary limits on European access to frontier AI models — specifically Anthropic's Mythos 5 and OpenAI's GPT 5.6 — apparently at the sole discretion of the Oval Office.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frontier-model access is now a discretionary geopolitical lever, not a market given; enterprises building on US frontier models outside the US face a new supply-continuity risk.
insights.quote: the US imposing temporary limits on access to the latest AI models – notably Anthropic’s Mythos 5 and OpenAI’s GPT 5.6 – apparently at the sole discretion of the Oval Office

insights.claim: The UK-US relationship is commercially entwined but normatively estranged, with the UK keeping its digital services tax and hardening online regulation despite US pressure, including a June 2026 ban on under-16s using social media that goes further than Australia's.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The UK is a durable regulatory hardliner on US tech; platform firms should price in tighter, not looser, UK compliance costs regardless of Washington's diplomatic leverage.
insights.quote: the two countries are commercially entwined but normatively estranged
insights.locator: The UK-US relationship has always had its difficulties

insights.claim: Middle powers including the UK, France and Germany are realizing that previously taken-for-granted access to US technology may be less stable or come with more strings, accelerating a broad sovereignty trend.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A structural sovereignty push across European middle powers creates demand for non-US and domestic tech stacks — a tailwind for European infrastructure, sovereign compute and local AI plays.
insights.quote: This friction has accelerated a broader trend across middle powers
insights.locator: This friction has accelerated a broader trend

themes.theme: Building AI leverage through physical capacity

insights.claim: Returns from cheap AI intelligence will be limited not by which models a country can access but by physical and industrial capacity — energy, grid connections, factory floors and lab capacity — to turn intelligence into output.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The AI value bottleneck is shifting to deployment infrastructure; energy, grid and industrial-capacity assets may capture more of the AI upside than model access itself.
insights.quote: The returns from cheap intelligence (in the form of AI) won’t be limited by which models the UK can access, but rather by the physical and industrial capacity to turn this cheap intelligence into output

insights.claim: The UK should prioritize safe use of NHS and other public data as a potentially decisive AI asset that fragmented, siloed systems and public-trust failures have kept locked up.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Unlocking NHS data could seed a differentiated UK AI health/bio research edge; watch policy moves on public-data access as a signal for UK life-sciences AI opportunity.
insights.quote: they remain a potentially decisive asset that years of fragmented, siloed systems and public trust failures have kept locked up
insights.locator: It should also prioritize routes to safely using NHS

insights.claim: The UK cannot and should not try to out-build the US or China on compute, but should secure enough sovereign onshore capacity for critical inference, with any surplus committed to start-ups, universities and a shared middle-power tech stack.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The realistic UK compute play is targeted sovereign inference capacity, not hyperscale training — a narrower, more investable niche than frontier data-centre buildout.
insights.quote: The right target is enough sovereign onshore capacity for critical inference – the compute used to deploy and use an AI model

themes.theme: Leading at home: digital identity and public services

insights.claim: Digital identity should be reframed from a civil-liberties liability into digital public infrastructure that eases everyday life and lays a foundation for public services and new digital businesses.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A UK policy shift toward digital ID as public infrastructure would open a new market layer for identity-linked public services and digital-business platforms.
insights.quote: Digital identity should be reframed for what it can and must be: a piece of digital public infrastructure that makes everyday life easier

insights.claim: Greater Manchester's AI and Data Innovation Office and Health Innovation Manchester's health-data work prove that modernising public services through digital technology is achievable.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
entities: Greater Manchester AI and Data Innovation Office
insights.so_what: Regional public-sector AI pilots are the template to scale nationally; vendors serving public-service AI and health data should track these as reference deployments.
insights.locator: The same logic applies to AI in public services

insights.claim: The UK's AI Security Institute gives it international standing in AI governance that capital cannot buy.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: UK leadership in AI safety/governance is a soft-power asset and potential standards-setting lever, influencing where global AI-governance norms and compliance markets form.
insights.quote: the UK’s AI Security Institute gives it international standing that capital cannot buy
insights.locator: Lastly, the UK should also build on its leadership

insights.claim: Maximum sovereignty comes from coalition with other countries rather than hopeless autarky, since dozens of countries share the UK's public-service technology needs and anxiety about entrenched provider dependencies.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A shared middle-power tech stack implies cross-border demand aggregation — an opening for interoperable, open, exportable public-sector technology rather than closed national silos.
insights.quote: The final priority is maximising sovereignty by coalition with other countries rather than hopeless attempts at autarky

insights.claim: The success of gov.uk — forked and used by many of Britain's allies — shows the UK's proven capacity to build technology that enables collaboration rather than shutting it off.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Exportable, forkable UK public-sector software is a credible model for capturing allied-market adoption; open digital public infrastructure is the strategic export, not proprietary silos.
insights.quote: The success of gov.uk – forked and used by many of Britain’s friends and allies – is just one example
insights.locator: To squander this opportunity by building rickety national silos
